upperAir: KOAK sounding shows a surface temp of 11.6°C with a moist layer near the surface (spread only 4.7°C at 1017mb), and a near-saturated layer at 850mb (spread 1.6°C at 1477m MSL), suggesting a shallow marine influence capping thermals below ~1500m. Lapse rate of 6.24°C/km surface to 850mb is sub-dry-adiabatic and on the stable side, meaning thermals will be weak and capped — BL heights of 800–1050m AGL at inland sites by early afternoon are plausible but modest. Strong westerlies aloft (250° at 25 kts at 850mb, 285° at 39 kts at 700mb) indicate significant wind shear above the boundary layer, which will dampen thermal development and keep soaring ceilings modest.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area/SF region. KSFO reporting 280–290° at 14–18G23 kts — strong afternoon sea breeze already established. KHAF persistent 330–340° at 16–25 kts, confirming robust NW flow at the coast with no marine layer — clear skies and strong onshore gradient. KRHV at 290° 12 kts, inland valley receiving the sea breeze punch. KAPC light at 310–350° 4–8 kts, suggesting weaker gradient north of the bay. KWVI SSW 7–9 kts at 18°C — Watsonville valley warm, south bay marine push light.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge launch, NW/N-facing, thermal and sea breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin 1750 is a thermal launch that also benefits from the afternoon sea breeze channeled through the Milpitas gap. Ideal conditions are a light N/NW morning flow transitioning to a steady NW sea breeze 8–15 kts by midday, with thermals climbing to 2000–3000 ft AGL. The site becomes unflyable when sea breeze over-pressures above 18–20 kts or thermals collapse under stable air.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a solid flyable day at Ed Levin 1750, with the NW sea breeze providing both ridge support and thermal triggering in a clean, dry airmass. The key story is a moderate, consistent NNW flow (HRRR and GFS fully agree: 8–10 kts surface, 10–13 kts at 80m) through the prime window, with BL heights building to 840–1050m MSL (~600–750 ft above launch) by midday — modest but workable thermals in the 2–4 mph range. The sounding's sub-dry-adiabatic lapse rate means thermals won't be screaming, but CAPE is near zero and skies are clear, so air is smooth and laminar. Wind direction locks in from 350–012° from late morning through afternoon — textbook on-face for this launch. The main watch is the late afternoon acceleration: by 1700–1800 local winds climb to 13 kts surface with 16+ kts at 80m, which starts pushing marginal for casual flying. No precip, no clouds, no AIRMETs.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light NNW at 8–10 kts surface, 10–13 kts at 80m, BL climbing from 395 to 735m. Thermals just beginning to fire — good window to launch and scratch up before the sea breeze fully fills in. Smooth conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind steady NNW/N at 9–10 kts surface, 10–11 kts at 80m — ideal window. BL tops out near 840–990m, supporting modest thermals with climbs likely to 2500–3200 ft MSL. Clean, organized air with negligible cloud cover.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze accelerates from 1600 onward: 12–13 kts surface, 14–16 kts at 80m by 1700. Still flyable for P3 but getting punchy on-face. P2 pilots should land by 1500 before the afternoon push. Conditions remain clear but wind energy increases.

verdict.hazards: Late afternoon wind acceleration to 13+ kts; thermals modest/capped under stable sounding — stay below 3500 ft MSL

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — lower training hill launch, NW-facing, thermal and beginner site
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch sits lower on the same ridge and is used for shorter flights and training. It benefits from the same NW sea breeze but thermals are weaker and shorter at this elevation. Ideal winds are 8–14 kts NW with stable, gentle thermals — this is a P2 learning site when conditions are mellow.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch sees nearly identical wind character to the upper launch today — NNW flow at 8–12 kts through the core window — but with a lower launch altitude the flyable airspace is shallower and thermals harder to work. BL heights reach 950–1025m by afternoon, giving meaningful height over the 600 ft LZ for thermal soaring if you can find the trigger. The site is well-suited for student and P2 flights through the early-to-mid afternoon. The same late afternoon acceleration concern applies: 15+ kts at 80m by 1700 is too punchy for beginners. Wind direction is consistently on-face (330–355°) through the prime window — no cross-wind concerns.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNW 8–9 kts, BL just beginning to build. Good light-wind morning window for P2 sled rides and early thermal attempts. Smooth, organized flow.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind steady NNW 8–9 kts surface, BL to 950–990m — prime P2 window. Gentle thermals accessible with patient searching. Clear skies, low stress.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sea breeze builds to 10–12 kts surface by 1600, with gusts to 15 kts at 80m by 1700. P2 pilots should wrap up by 1430–1500. P3 can continue but expect increasing turbulence near the ridge.

verdict.hazards: Afternoon sea breeze acceleration; shallow thermals under stable cap — conservative altitude management required

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W/NW-facing ridge soaring site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge soaring site powered by onshore NW sea breeze against the coastal bluff. The ideal window is a steady 10–18 kts NW/WNW flow with clear skies or high cloud — no marine layer overhead. It goes big in strong NW flow but becomes dangerous if wind exceeds 20–22 kts or rotor develops below the bluff.
sites.todaySetup: Today is a legitimate FLY day at Mussel Rock, driven by a building NW sea breeze that fills in nicely by midday and afternoon. The deciding factor: KHAF METARs are confirming 330–340° at 16–25 kts already this afternoon — that's the coastal gradient right at the source, and the buoy at Half Moon Bay shows 19.4 kts from 310° with SST 13.0°C. The site winds lag KHAF slightly, and GFS/HRRR show Mussel Rock surface winds at 7–12 kts NW through the core window (10 AM–4 PM), building to 13–14 kts by 1600–1700 — squarely in the sweet spot for ridge soaring. No marine layer (KHAF CLR, buoy pressure high at 1021 hPa), meaning the bluff is in clean air. The one caution is that the coastal winds are already strong at KHAF and could push the actual site above the GFS numbers — treat 14 kts on-site as the soft upper limit for P2 and be ready to land if gusts stack up. Late afternoon 14–19 kts at 80m warrants attention.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light to moderate NW build, 4–8 kts surface at the site. May be marginal for sustained ridge soaring early — monitor and wait for the sea breeze to fill. KHAF already strong so the gradient is there.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Sweet spot: 10–12 kts NW surface, 13–15 kts at 80m. Ridge lift consistent and organized. Clear skies confirmed. Prime P2/P3 window — classic Mussel Rock soaring conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds to 13–14 kts surface, 17–20 kts at 80m by 1600–1700. Getting strong for P2 but P3 pilots in their element. Monitor KHAF for gusts above 22 kts as a bail signal.

verdict.hazards: Coastal gradient strong — afternoon wind acceleration possible above GFS forecast; rotor below bluff if wind exceeds 20 kts; SST 13°C confirms deep marine layer potential on lighter days (not an issue today)

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland peak, NW/N-facing, thermal and sea breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak launches face NW and rely on a combination of valley thermals and the afternoon Altamont/bay sea breeze push. Ideal conditions are a moderate NNW sea breeze 8–16 kts with good thermal development. The site can get rowdy when the sea breeze over-pressures or thermals are strong and punchy.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares nearly identical atmospheric forcing with Ed Levin today — same GFS/HRRR model data, same NNW sea breeze, same BL heights — and it flies well. The wind holds steady NNW 9–13 kts from launch through the prime window, with BL tops reaching 840–895m by midday and afternoon, supporting climbs to approximately 3000 ft MSL. Wind direction from 350–012° is on-face for the NW launch throughout the window. The sounding's modest lapse rate keeps thermals organized rather than punchy — good for P2/P3 pilots wanting a smooth but productive flight. The same late-afternoon watch applies: winds climb to 13 kts surface and 16+ kts at 80m by 1700 local, which is the upper comfortable limit for most pilots. No precipitation, no clouds, no AIRMETs — a clean inland thermal day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNW 8–10 kts, BL building from 395 to 735m. Thermals just triggering — good launch window, expect light-moderate climbs and smooth air.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Optimal window: NNW 9–10 kts, BL to 840m, consistent on-face flow. Thermals moderate, tops around 2800–3200 ft MSL. Clear skies, organized conditions — best flying of the day.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind acceleration begins around 1600: 12–13 kts surface, 14–16 kts at 80m. Still flyable for P3 but increasingly punchy. P2 pilots land by 1430–1500 to stay in their comfort envelope.

verdict.hazards: Late afternoon sea breeze acceleration; thermals capped at modest heights under stable sounding — watch for rotor in lee terrain features

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Friday April 3 looks like a NO FLY or MARGINAL day. Both GFS and HRRR show NE/ENE winds at 14–17 kts surface and 20–25 kts at 80m — wrong direction for the NW-facing launch and well above comfortable limits. BL is suppressed at 160–420m, thermals essentially absent. Tomorrow.io shows gusts to 25–29 kts. Avoid.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Same story as the upper launch on Friday — NE winds 19–22 kts surface, 23–30 kts at 80m, BL suppressed. This is a strong offshore/drainage flow event. NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Friday is a NO FLY day at Mussel Rock. GFS/HRRR show light and variable to E winds with near-calm surface flow in the morning, then confused/light afternoon. Tomorrow.io suggests NNW 8–10 kts with gusts only to 14 kts — insufficient for reliable ridge lift, and cloud cover near 100% by afternoon. Not worth the trip.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Friday is NO FLY at Mission Peak. Same NE/ENE offshore flow at 14–17 kts surface, 20–25 kts at 80m — wrong direction and too strong. BL suppressed. Skip.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday April 4 looks dramatically improved — light and variable winds (1–6 kts), BL heights building strongly to 1265–1620m by afternoon. This is a classic spring thermal day with weak gradient. Potential for strong thermals and good cross-country conditions, but also potential for overdevelopment in weak-gradient airmass. Watch for afternoon Cu development.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Saturday follows the same pattern — calm morning, light SW/NW 5–6 kts afternoon, BL to 1265–1620m. Excellent thermal soaring potential if convection stays organized. Good P2/P3 day with patient flying.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Saturday April 4 shows E/SE winds in the morning (onshore side is dead) shifting to light N/NW by afternoon, with only 3–5 kts. BL heights modest. Likely not enough sea breeze to sustain ridge soaring — light thermal day at best. Marginal.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Saturday looks very promising — light variable flow, BL heights reaching 1240–1275m by afternoon. Strong thermal development likely. Good XC potential for P3 pilots. P2 should assess conditions on site as thermals may become punchy.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No Day 3 (April 5) data available in current forecast window. Extrapolating from the post-offshore pattern, expect a gradual return of the NW sea breeze. Monitor updated forecasts.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as upper launch — Day 3 beyond model window. Check updated briefing.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 beyond reliable forecast window. If NW sea breeze re-establishes after the Saturday thermal day, Sunday could offer ridge soaring again. Monitor buoy and KHAF trends.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 beyond current data window. Saturday's thermal pattern may persist into Sunday if gradient stays weak. Check updated model runs.

watchlist: Late afternoon sea breeze acceleration at all inland sites today — winds climb to 13 kts surface / 16+ kts at 80m after 1600; P2 pilots should land by 1430
KHAF coastal winds already at 18–25 kts this afternoon — Mussel Rock could run stronger than GFS surface numbers suggest; monitor on-site before committing
Friday April 3 offshore/NE flow event: all sites likely NO FLY — NE winds 14–22 kts at the surface with 20–30 kts at 80m and suppressed BL
Saturday April 4 light-gradient thermal day has strong XC potential but watch for afternoon overdevelopment under weak synoptic setup
SST at Half Moon Bay buoy is 13.0°C — relatively cold water reinforces strong marine layer potential on future light-wind days; not an issue today with clear coastal skies
tldr: Today is the pick of the week — all four sites are flyable with a clean NNW sea breeze, clear skies, and no AIRMETs. Best window is 10:30 AM to 2:30 PM at Ed Levin or Mission Peak for thermals, or 11 AM–3 PM at Mussel Rock for ridge soaring. Friday is a skip (offshore NE flow event), so get your flights in today.
